meta.title: 14.6 Capstone
meta.description: Design a complete system from scratch — applying every concept from the entire System Design Mastery course.

blocks.content:
## The Final Challenge

This is it. You have spent 14 levels building your system design toolkit — from back-of-envelope estimation to distributed consensus, from caching strategies to architecture reviews. Now you will apply all of it.

The challenge below is intentionally open-ended. There is no single correct answer. What matters is your reasoning, your tradeoffs, and your ability to justify every decision.

blocks.title:
Design a global real-time collaboration platform (like Google Docs or Figma) from scratch. The platform must support: (1) Real-time collaborative editing where multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously with sub-200ms latency worldwide, (2) Document storage and versioning with the ability to view and restore any previous version, (3) Offline support — users can edit without internet and sync when reconnected, (4) 50 million registered users, 5 million daily active users, peak of 500,000 concurrent editing sessions, (5) Rich media support — documents can contain images, embedded videos, and tables, (6) Sharing and permissions — owner, editor, commenter, viewer roles with link sharing.

Your design should address ALL of the following:

**Architecture:** High-level system diagram. What services exist? How do they communicate? Monolith, microservices, or hybrid? Justify your choice.

**Real-time collaboration:** How do you handle concurrent edits? OT (Operational Transformation) vs. CRDTs? How do you resolve conflicts? What protocol do you use (WebSockets, WebRTC, SSE)?

**Data layer:** What databases do you use and why? How is document data modeled? How do you handle versioning (event sourcing, snapshot + deltas, full copies)? What is your sharding strategy?

**Offline and sync:** How does offline editing work? How do you merge offline changes with changes made by other users while the user was offline? What happens with conflicting edits?

**Global distribution:** How do you achieve sub-200ms latency worldwide? Where do you place servers? How do you handle the consistency vs. latency tradeoff for a collaborative editing system?

**Reliability and failure handling:** What happens when a collaboration server crashes mid-session? How do you handle network partitions between regions? What is your disaster recovery plan?

**Cost estimation:** Estimate monthly cloud costs for compute, storage, and network at the given scale. What are the biggest cost drivers and how do you optimize them?

**Team and organizational design:** How would you structure the engineering teams to build and operate this system? What team topologies would you use?

**Migration plan:** If this were replacing an existing simpler system (e.g., a basic document editor without real-time collaboration), how would you migrate users and data?
blocks.solution:
There is no single correct answer, but a strong design would include:

**Architecture:** Microservices or hybrid approach. Core services: Collaboration Service (real-time editing engine), Document Service (CRUD, versioning, storage), Auth/Permissions Service, Media Service (image/video processing and CDN), Presence Service (who is online, cursor positions), Notification Service. Communication: WebSockets for real-time editing, gRPC for inter-service, Kafka for async events.

**Real-time collaboration:** CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) are the modern choice over OT. CRDTs guarantee eventual consistency without a central server, which is essential for offline support. Specifically, a sequence CRDT like Yjs or Automerge for text editing. WebSocket connections to regional collaboration servers. Each server maintains the CRDT state for active documents. Changes are broadcast to all connected clients and persisted asynchronously.

**Data layer:** PostgreSQL for user accounts, permissions, and document metadata (relational, ACID). S3 for document content and media (cheap, durable). Redis for active session state and presence (fast, ephemeral). Document versioning via event sourcing — store every operation. Periodic snapshots (every 100 operations) for fast loading. Shard documents by document ID (hash-based). User data sharded by user ID.

**Offline and sync:** CRDTs handle this naturally. The client maintains a local CRDT replica. Edits are applied locally and queued for sync. On reconnection, the client sends its queued operations. The CRDT merge algorithm guarantees convergence regardless of operation order. No manual conflict resolution needed — this is the key advantage of CRDTs over OT.

**Global distribution:** Collaboration servers in 3+ regions (US, EU, Asia). A user connects to the nearest region. For a shared document with users in multiple regions, one region is the 'home' region (where the document was created or where most collaborators are). Cross-region sync via Kafka with 50-100ms replication lag. Total latency: ~50ms local + ~100ms cross-region sync = 150ms for global collaboration. CDN (CloudFront) for media assets.

**Reliability:** Collaboration servers are stateless — CRDT state is reconstructable from the event log. If a server crashes, clients reconnect to another server, which rebuilds state from the last snapshot + recent events. Kafka provides durability for the event stream. Multi-region replication means a full region failure loses no data. RTO: minutes (failover to another region). RPO: near-zero (async replication lag only).

**Cost estimation (monthly):** Compute — 500K concurrent sessions, ~1000 WebSocket connections per server = 500 servers (c6i.xlarge at $124/mo reserved) = $62,000. Storage — 50M users × average 100 documents × 50KB average = 250TB on S3 = $5,750. Plus versioning overhead (3x) = $17,250. Network — WebSocket traffic: 500K sessions × 1KB/sec average = 500MB/sec = 1.3PB/month egress = ~$40,000 (with CDN optimization). Kafka cluster: 10 brokers = $12,000. PostgreSQL RDS: Multi-AZ, 3 read replicas = $15,000. Redis cluster: $8,000. Total: ~$155,000/month. Biggest cost drivers: compute (WebSocket servers) and network egress. Optimize with connection multiplexing, delta compression, and aggressive CDN caching for media.

**Team topology:** Stream-aligned teams: Document Editing Team (CRDT engine, collaboration protocol), Document Management Team (storage, versioning, search), Sharing and Permissions Team, Media Team (upload, processing, CDN). Platform team: Infrastructure (Kubernetes, CI/CD, monitoring). Enabling team: Real-time Systems specialists (help teams with WebSocket, CRDT expertise). Total: ~40-60 engineers.

**Migration plan:** Strangler fig pattern. Phase 1: New editor for new documents only, old editor for existing documents. Phase 2: Background migration of existing documents to new format (batch job). Phase 3: Dual-mode — existing documents open in new editor with automatic format conversion on first access. Phase 4: Deprecate old editor. Data migration via ETL with format transformation. Rollback: keep old system running for 3 months after full migration.

blocks.content:
## Congratulations

If you completed this capstone with a thoughtful, well-reasoned design that addresses architecture, data, reliability, cost, and organizational structure — you have developed the architect's intuition. You do not just know the concepts; you can apply them together to solve ambiguous, open-ended problems.

The real world will always be messier than any exercise. Requirements change, teams disagree, systems fail in unexpected ways. But with the mental models from this course, you have the foundation to navigate that complexity and build systems that work at scale.
